name: Prashastapada
dates_notes: Dated by being cited in Dignāga (sixth century) and citing earlier Vaiśeṣika materials; Halbfass (1992) and Bronkhorst converge on the late fifth to sixth century CE. Praśastapāda is named in colophons but otherwise biographically unknown.

lineage_polemical.note: Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.11–17 engages the developed Vaiśeṣika position represented by Praśastapāda's Padārtha-Dharma-Saṅgraha, not merely Kaṇāda's bare sūtras; the refutation operates against the classical theistic-cosmological articulation.

Praśastapāda's classical Vaiśeṣika position is the framework Udayana later defends and reformulates against the Buddhist pramāṇa-tradition; the engagement is constructive within Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika and oppositional ad extra.

core_thesis: Praśastapāda's *Padārtha-Dharma-Saṅgraha* — also called the *Praśastapāda-Bhāṣya*, though it is a free re-presentation rather than a *sūtra*-by-*sūtra* commentary — is the classical articulation of Vaiśeṣika and the work in which the school's mature shape is fixed. The work re-organizes Kaṇāda's six *padārthas* under a new analytical scheme, treating each *padārtha* as bearing its own *dharmas* (defining properties) which the text systematically lists. Three doctrinal moves give the work its philosophical weight. First, *īśvara* is introduced as the conscious initiator of the first *paramāṇu*-conjunction at *sarga* and the conscious instrument of dissolution at *pralaya* (*Padārtha-Dharma-Saṅgraha*, *sṛṣṭi-saṃhāra-prakaraṇa*, in the *dravya-padārtha-nirūpaṇa* on *paramāṇu*) — the older atheistic Vaiśeṣika of the bare *sūtras* becomes a theistic cosmology. Second, the *padārtha*-grid is given the analytical apparatus that lets it function as a complete ontology: each *guṇa* gets its substance-locus (*guṇa-padārtha-nirūpaṇa*, listing twenty-four *guṇas*), each *karma* its trajectory (*karma-padārtha-nirūpaṇa*, listing five *karmas*), each *sāmānya* its ranked position in the *para-* / *apara-* hierarchy (*sāmānya-padārtha-nirūpaṇa*, distinguishing *sattā* as *para-sāmānya*). Third — disputed in modern scholarship as to whether it is Praśastapāda's own innovation or that of his commentators (Śrīdhara, Udayana) — the seventh category *abhāva* (absence) emerges as a candidate addition to the canonical six (in the closing *abhāva-prakaraṇa*; cf. Śrīdhara's *Nyāya-Kandalī ad loc.*). The resulting *padārtha-dharma-saṅgraha* is the framework Udayana inherits and that Navya-Nyāya later rebuilds.

engaged_works.title: Padartha-Dharma-Sangraha
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Dated by being cited in Dignāga and the developed Buddhist pramāṇa-literature.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Praśastapāda by colophon and by uniform citation across Śrīdhara's Nyāya-Kandalī, Udayana's Kiraṇāvalī, and Vyomaśiva's Vyomavatī.

ascription_evidence.description: Halbfass (1992, On Being and What There Is); Matilal (Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika); Bronkhorst on the dating-anchor through Dignāga.

engaged_works.summary: A free systematic exposition of Vaiśeṣika in prose, organized by *padārtha*. The opening section (*maṅgala* and *prayojana-prakaraṇa*) gives a brief restatement of the school's purpose. The first major division (*dravya-padārtha-nirūpaṇa*) treats *dravya*: each of the nine substances (earth, water, fire, air, *ākāśa*, *kāla*, *dik*, *ātman*, *manas*) is defined by its characteristic *guṇas* and *karmas*; the *paramāṇu*-doctrine is unfolded as the analytical-recombinative cosmology (in the *pṛthivī-* and *paramāṇu-prakaraṇa*), with *īśvara* introduced as the conscious initiator of *sarga* and *pralaya* in the *sṛṣṭi-saṃhāra-prakaraṇa*. The second division (*guṇa-padārtha-nirūpaṇa*) treats *guṇa* and gives the canonical Vaiśeṣika list of twenty-four qualities (later expanded). The third division (*karma-padārtha-nirūpaṇa*) treats *karma* and lists the five *karmas* (*utkṣepaṇa*, *avakṣepaṇa*, *ākuñcana*, *prasāraṇa*, *gamana*). The fourth division (*sāmānya-padārtha-nirūpaṇa*) treats *sāmānya*, distinguishing *para-sāmānya* (the highest universal, *sattā*) from *apara-sāmānya*; *viśeṣa* (*viśeṣa-padārtha-nirūpaṇa*) and *samavāya* (*samavāya-padārtha-nirūpaṇa*) follow. The text concludes with epistemology in the *buddhi-prakaraṇa*: the *pramāṇa*-list is reduced to *pratyakṣa* and *anumāna*, with *śabda* subsumed under *anumāna*. The principal pre-modern commentaries are Śrīdhara's *Nyāya-Kandalī* (tenth century) and Udayana's *Kiraṇāvalī* (eleventh century).
